source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Compare how things move on different surfaces; notice that some forces need contact between two objects but magnetic forces can act at a distance; observe how magnets attract or repel each other and attract some materials and not others; compare and group materials by magnetic attraction; describe magnets as having two poles; and predict whether two magnets will attract or repel.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack develops evidence-led push, pull, surface and magnet reasoning through controlled virtual tests. It uses a simple interaction model, avoids claiming that all metals are magnetic, and distinguishes magnetic attraction from ordinary sticking or gravity.

objective.child_goal: I can test how pushes, pulls, surfaces and magnets affect objects and explain what the evidence shows.
objective.statement: Compare motion on surfaces, distinguish contact and non-contact interactions, classify magnetic materials and predict attraction or repulsion from pole orientation.
misconceptions: Thinks a force is used up after a push or pull and cannot describe its effect on motion.
Assumes rough surfaces always make objects move faster because they provide more grip.
Thinks every metal is attracted to a magnet or that non-metal objects can never contain magnetic material.
Thinks magnets attract every object, or that any two magnet ends will attract regardless of pole orientation.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A push or pull that can change an object's movement or shape.
vocabulary.audio_script: A force is a push or pull. We notice it by its effect.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A labelled push or pull arrow beside a before-and-after object.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A push or pull that happens when objects touch.
vocabulary.audio_script: A contact force needs the objects to touch.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A hand touching a cart with a contact badge.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A push or pull from a magnet that can act without touching.
vocabulary.audio_script: Magnetic force can attract or repel across a gap.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Two magnets separated by a visible gap and force arrows.

vocabulary.audio_script: When magnets attract, they pull towards each other.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Two labelled poles with arrows pointing together.

vocabulary.audio_script: When magnets repel, they push away from each other.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Two matching poles with arrows pointing apart.

vocabulary.child_explanation: One of the two ends of a magnet, called north or south.
vocabulary.audio_script: Every magnet has a north pole and a south pole.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A bar magnet with N and S labels, patterns and spoken names.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: For each picture, choose push or pull and name what changed: start, stop, speed, direction or shape.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect force language to observable effects before comparing specific interactions.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Look for the effect. The object may start, stop, speed up, slow down, turn or change shape.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Paired still frames show before and after with a labelled effect menu.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort interactions into NEEDS TOUCH and CAN ACT ACROSS A GAP.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Distinguish contact forces from the observed non-contact action of magnets.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A hand must touch the cart, but a magnet can move a magnetic object across a small gap.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A contact icon and a measured-gap icon label each interaction.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: The same toy car is released from the same ramp onto smooth card and rough fabric. Compare the distances.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model a fair surface test and avoid explaining results from surface labels alone.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Keep the car and release point the same. Change the surface, measure the distance and compare repeated trials.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Two tracks share car and ramp-height labels, with ruler and repeat-results table.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Test one object at a time, record whether it is attracted, then group the materials using your results.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Build material classification from observations rather than appearance or the word metal.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Predict, test across a small gap, record the result, and use the evidence to group the objects.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A safe virtual magnet, mixed everyday objects, gap marker and results table.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Predict what happens when north faces north, then turn one magnet so north faces south.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check pole-based attraction and repulsion instead of memorising that magnets always pull.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Matching poles repel. Different poles attract.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: N and S labels remain visible while direction arrows and the gap change.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach the companion why 'shiny' or 'metal' is not enough evidence that an object will be attracted.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Secure cautious classification and the need for a test result.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Name the material if known, describe the magnet test and use the result in your conclusion.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Prediction and observation cards stay separate until the conclusion is spoken.

manipulatives.purpose: Compare how a constant object moves over different surfaces using repeatable releases and measurements.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners choose one changed surface, lock the car and release point, run three trials, record distance and select a cautious pattern statement.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: The moving car has step-frame and no-motion alternatives; results are announced and shown in a table; controls use buttons and keyboard steps; ruler readings have large text and audio; no speed scoring or fine dragging is required.

manipulatives.purpose: Test attraction across a gap, classify objects by evidence and compare magnet pole orientations.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners position an object or second magnet using buttons, reveal pole labels, observe attract/no-attract/repel and record each result before concluding.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: All spatial actions have linear text equivalents; N and S use letters, patterns and spoken names; direction is written as towards or away; reduced-motion mode uses before-and-after panels; the simulation avoids loose real magnets and ingestion hazards.

practice.interleaving_notes: Establish push/pull effects and touch-versus-gap distinctions first; then mix surface evidence, material tests and pole predictions while keeping prediction, observation and conclusion separate.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Identify pushes and pulls, observable effects and whether contact is required.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: before/after scene + push/pull choice + effect label + touch/gap classification
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Do not infer unshown forces from a single still image; use safe, familiar actions and distinguish force from movement.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Compare movement over surfaces using a controlled setup, repeated distance evidence and cautious conclusions.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: same object/release + changed surface + three distance trials + table comparison + evidence statement
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Generated data must be internally consistent; avoid universal smooth-versus-rough claims beyond the tested object and conditions.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Predict, test and group materials by observed attraction to a magnet.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: object with stated material + predict + across-gap test + attracted/not-attracted record + group conclusion
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include iron or steel examples and non-attracted metals such as aluminium or copper; avoid uncertain composites unless construction is stated.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Predict and explain attraction or repulsion from the facing north and south poles.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: two labelled magnet orientations + attract/repel/no-effect prediction + direction arrows + turn-one-magnet comparison
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Pole labels must remain visible and colour-independent; do not imply a single isolated magnetic pole can be cut from a magnet.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Retain contact, surface, material and pole reasoning through concise mixed evidence tasks.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: review queue weighted to errors + static setup or data row + prediction/observation/conclusion choice
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep reading demand below the science demand and never recommend unsupervised testing with small strong magnets or electronic devices.

body.prompt: The same car is released from the same point. Which conclusion fits the results?
choices: In this test, the car travelled farther on smooth card than on rough fabric
Rough fabric always makes every object move faster
The car changed size on the fabric
The release point caused the difference because it changed each time

interaction_route.touch: Tap a labelled model part, table row or numbered choice; buttons and steppers replace precise object movement.
interaction_route.keyboard: Tab through the model or evidence table and choices; use arrows for labelled states and Enter or Space to select and check.
interaction_route.switch_scan: Scan prompt, static model/table, numbered choices, check and retry in a fixed order with one activation per decision.
interaction_route.eye_gaze: Use large dwell-select model labels and choices with adjustable dwell time and a confirm step.
interaction_route.aac_point_adult_recorded: The learner may point, use AAC or direct an adult to select or record the indicated prediction, evidence or explanation without the adult supplying the science decision.

body.accessible_response_route: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-recorded responses provide equivalent science evidence; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never mandatory.
body.force_model_route: Labelled before/after still frames show object, push/pull or measured gap and observable change in speed, direction, position or shape; a linear text description is equivalent.
body.surface_fair_test_route: CHANGE SURFACE, KEEP SAME and MEASURE DISTANCE cards sit beside repeated-results rows; motion can be replaced by static release and finish frames.
body.magnetic_material_route: Prediction and observation remain separate; object, known material, fixed test gap and attracted/not-attracted result are text-labelled without relying on shine or colour.
body.pole_model_route: N and S use letters, patterns and spoken-name text; facing poles and towards/away arrows have a linear pair-and-outcome alternative.
body.evidence_table_route: One evidence row appears at a time with repeat values, controlled variables and a cautious conclusion stem; correct evidence remains visible during repair.
body.sensory_safe_route: No sudden sound, flashing, compulsory motion or handling of real magnets; static virtual models avoid loose-magnet and ingestion risks.
body.reduced_load_route: Reveal prediction, one model/test variable, observation and conclusion separately while preserving correct work and labels.
body.supported_interaction: select a labelled choice or enter the response with large, focusable controls. Equivalent routes include touch, keyboard, single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC or pointing, and adult-recorded responses directed by the learner; no handwriting, speech or fine drag is mandatory.

expected_answer.value: In this test, the car travelled farther on smooth card than on rough fabric

hints: Compare all three results for each surface.
Choose a conclusion limited to this test.
question_variants.explanation: The repeated measurements are close together and show a greater distance on smooth card in this controlled setup.

feedback.correct: “In this test, the car travelled farther on smooth card than on rough fabric” matches the repeated distance evidence from a test where the surface changed and the object, release point and measuring method stayed controlled.
feedback.repair: Return to the fair-test table. Check that only surface changed, compare all repeated distances for the same object and release point, and state ‘in this test’ rather than using the surface label to guess an always-rule.
feedback.science_evidence: Repeated distances: smooth_card: 82, 80, 81 cm; rough_fabric: 39, 41, 40 cm. These support “In this test, the car travelled farther on smooth card than on rough fabric” within the controlled setup.
feedback.misconception_support: rough_means_faster: Return to the fair-test table. Check that only surface changed, compare all repeated distances for the same object and release point, and state ‘in this test’ rather than using the surface label to guess an always-rule.
feedback.model_or_fair_test_support: Use CHANGE SURFACE → KEEP OBJECT/RELEASE/MEASURE SAME → REPEAT → COMPARE DISTANCES → CAUTIOUS CONCLUSION.
feedback.support_message: Static models, tables, touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult recording are equally valid; speed, fine dragging, speech and handwriting are not scored.
feedback.retry: Your correct prediction, controlled variable, observation or pole label stays. Inspect one evidence clue and retry without losing progress.

expected_answer.value: A magnet attracting a steel paperclip

hints: Three examples need objects to touch.
A magnetic force can act across a gap.
question_variants.explanation: The magnet can attract the steel paperclip before touching it; the other examples are contact forces.

feedback.correct: “A magnet attracting a steel paperclip” uses the observed interaction and effect: identify push, pull or magnetic action, then describe what changed rather than treating force as stored or used up.
feedback.repair: Identify the interaction first: did objects touch, and was it a push or pull, or did a magnet act across a gap? Then compare before and after to name the change in movement, direction or shape; a force is an interaction, not a substance that runs out.
feedback.science_evidence: Which interaction can make an object move across a small gap without touching it? The contact/gap and before–after evidence supports “A magnet attracting a steel paperclip”.
feedback.misconception_support: force_used_up: Identify the interaction first: did objects touch, and was it a push or pull, or did a magnet act across a gap? Then compare before and after to name the change in movement, direction or shape; a force is an interaction, not a substance that runs out.
feedback.model_or_fair_test_support: Use TOUCH OR GAP → PUSH/PULL/MAGNETIC → BEFORE/AFTER EFFECT. Keep movement as an effect, not the force itself.
feedback.support_message: Static models, tables, touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult recording are equally valid; speed, fine dragging, speech and handwriting are not scored.
feedback.retry: Your correct prediction, controlled variable, observation or pole label stays. Inspect one evidence clue and retry without losing progress.

expected_answer.value: Some metals are attracted to this magnet and some are not

hints: Use all three test results.
Steel, aluminium and copper are metals, but their results differ.
question_variants.explanation: The steel object was attracted, while the aluminium and copper objects were not, so the evidence does not support an all-metals rule.

feedback.correct: “Some metals are attracted to this magnet and some are not” follows the attracted/not-attracted test result and does not classify from shininess or the word metal alone.
feedback.repair: Separate prediction from observation, keep the same magnet and gap, and group each object by its recorded attracted/not-attracted result. Some metals, such as iron and many steels, are attracted; aluminium, copper and brass are not in these tests.
feedback.science_evidence: The steel object was attracted, while the aluminium and copper objects were not, so the evidence does not support an all-metals rule. The conclusion uses all shown material results.
feedback.misconception_support: all_metals_magnetic: Separate prediction from observation, keep the same magnet and gap, and group each object by its recorded attracted/not-attracted result. Some metals, such as iron and many steels, are attracted; aluminium, copper and brass are not in these tests.
feedback.model_or_fair_test_support: Use PREDICT → SAME MAGNET/GAP TEST → RECORD ATTRACTED OR NOT → GROUP FROM EVIDENCE, NOT APPEARANCE.
feedback.support_message: Static models, tables, touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult recording are equally valid; speed, fine dragging, speech and handwriting are not scored.
feedback.retry: Your correct prediction, controlled variable, observation or pole label stays. Inspect one evidence clue and retry without losing progress.

expected_answer.value: They repel

hints: The facing poles match.
Matching poles push away.
question_variants.explanation: Two north poles are matching poles, so the magnets repel and move away if free to move.

feedback.correct: “They repel” follows the labelled facing poles: matching poles repel and different poles attract across a gap.
feedback.repair: Read only the two facing pole labels after any turn: N–N or S–S are matching and repel; N–S or S–N are different and attract. Match repel with away arrows and attract with towards arrows.
feedback.science_evidence: N faces N; matching poles repel, supporting “They repel”.
feedback.misconception_support: magnets_always_attract: Read only the two facing pole labels after any turn: N–N or S–S are matching and repel; N–S or S–N are different and attract. Match repel with away arrows and attract with towards arrows.
feedback.model_or_fair_test_support: Use FACE LABELS → MATCHING OR DIFFERENT → REPEL/AWAY OR ATTRACT/TOWARDS → CHECK AFTER ANY TURN.
feedback.support_message: Static models, tables, touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult recording are equally valid; speed, fine dragging, speech and handwriting are not scored.
feedback.retry: Your correct prediction, controlled variable, observation or pole label stays. Inspect one evidence clue and retry without losing progress.

Record it as attracted because it looks useful
Change the prediction instead of recording the result
Group it by shininess only
body.keyboard_instructions: Use arrow keys to review choices, then Enter to select.

interaction_route.touch: Tap a labelled model part, table row or numbered choice; buttons and steppers replace precise object movement.
interaction_route.keyboard: Tab through the model or evidence table and choices; use arrows for labelled states and Enter or Space to select and check.
interaction_route.switch_scan: Scan prompt, static model/table, numbered choices, check and retry in a fixed order with one activation per decision.
interaction_route.eye_gaze: Use large dwell-select model labels and choices with adjustable dwell time and a confirm step.
interaction_route.aac_point_adult_recorded: The learner may point, use AAC or direct an adult to select or record the indicated prediction, evidence or explanation without the adult supplying the science decision.

body.accessible_response_route: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-recorded responses provide equivalent science evidence; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never mandatory.
body.force_model_route: Labelled before/after still frames show object, push/pull or measured gap and observable change in speed, direction, position or shape; a linear text description is equivalent.
body.surface_fair_test_route: CHANGE SURFACE, KEEP SAME and MEASURE DISTANCE cards sit beside repeated-results rows; motion can be replaced by static release and finish frames.
body.magnetic_material_route: Prediction and observation remain separate; object, known material, fixed test gap and attracted/not-attracted result are text-labelled without relying on shine or colour.
body.pole_model_route: N and S use letters, patterns and spoken-name text; facing poles and towards/away arrows have a linear pair-and-outcome alternative.
body.evidence_table_route: One evidence row appears at a time with repeat values, controlled variables and a cautious conclusion stem; correct evidence remains visible during repair.
body.sensory_safe_route: No sudden sound, flashing, compulsory motion or handling of real magnets; static virtual models avoid loose-magnet and ingestion risks.
body.reduced_load_route: Reveal prediction, one model/test variable, observation and conclusion separately while preserving correct work and labels.

expected_answer.value: Record the ceramic tile in the not-attracted group

hints: Record what the test showed, even if the prediction differed.
Group by attraction evidence rather than appearance.
question_variants.explanation: Record the ceramic tile in the not-attracted group because the observation, not the prediction, determines the evidence group.

feedback.correct: “Record the ceramic tile in the not-attracted group” follows the attracted/not-attracted test result and does not classify from shininess or the word metal alone.
feedback.repair: Separate prediction from observation, keep the same magnet and gap, and group each object by its recorded attracted/not-attracted result. Some metals, such as iron and many steels, are attracted; aluminium, copper and brass are not in these tests.
feedback.science_evidence: ceramic tile (ceramic) was not_attracted at the same small test gap, supporting “Record the ceramic tile in the not-attracted group”.
feedback.misconception_support: all_metals_magnetic: Separate prediction from observation, keep the same magnet and gap, and group each object by its recorded attracted/not-attracted result. Some metals, such as iron and many steels, are attracted; aluminium, copper and brass are not in these tests.
feedback.model_or_fair_test_support: Use PREDICT → SAME MAGNET/GAP TEST → RECORD ATTRACTED OR NOT → GROUP FROM EVIDENCE, NOT APPEARANCE.
feedback.support_message: Static models, tables, touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult recording are equally valid; speed, fine dragging, speech and handwriting are not scored.
feedback.retry: Your correct prediction, controlled variable, observation or pole label stays. Inspect one evidence clue and retry without losing progress.

expected_answer.value: They repel

hints: Read both facing pole letters.
Matching poles repel.
question_variants.explanation: N and N are matching poles, so the magnets repel.

feedback.correct: “They repel” follows the labelled facing poles: matching poles repel and different poles attract across a gap.
feedback.repair: Read only the two facing pole labels after any turn: N–N or S–S are matching and repel; N–S or S–N are different and attract. Match repel with away arrows and attract with towards arrows.
feedback.science_evidence: N faces N; matching poles repel, supporting “They repel”.
feedback.misconception_support: magnets_always_attract: Read only the two facing pole labels after any turn: N–N or S–S are matching and repel; N–S or S–N are different and attract. Match repel with away arrows and attract with towards arrows.
feedback.model_or_fair_test_support: Use FACE LABELS → MATCHING OR DIFFERENT → REPEL/AWAY OR ATTRACT/TOWARDS → CHECK AFTER ANY TURN.
feedback.support_message: Static models, tables, touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult recording are equally valid; speed, fine dragging, speech and handwriting are not scored.
feedback.retry: Your correct prediction, controlled variable, observation or pole label stays. Inspect one evidence clue and retry without losing progress.

expected_answer.value: The test evidence separates these two metal materials

hints: Use both observations, including the object that was not attracted.
Avoid making a rule about every object from two tests.
question_variants.explanation: The test evidence separates these two metal materials. This conclusion stays within the observed results.

feedback.correct: “The test evidence separates these two metal materials” follows the attracted/not-attracted test result and does not classify from shininess or the word metal alone.
feedback.repair: Separate prediction from observation, keep the same magnet and gap, and group each object by its recorded attracted/not-attracted result. Some metals, such as iron and many steels, are attracted; aluminium, copper and brass are not in these tests.
feedback.science_evidence: The test evidence separates these two metal materials. This conclusion stays within the observed results. The conclusion uses all shown material results.
feedback.misconception_support: all_metals_magnetic: Separate prediction from observation, keep the same magnet and gap, and group each object by its recorded attracted/not-attracted result. feedback.model_or_fair_test_support: Use PREDICT → SAME MAGNET/GAP TEST → RECORD ATTRACTED OR NOT → GROUP FROM EVIDENCE, NOT APPEARANCE.
feedback.support_message: Static models, tables, touch, keyboard, switch, eye gaze, AAC, pointing and adult recording are equally valid; speed, fine dragging, speech and handwriting are not scored.

Matching poles push away from each other.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks a force is used up after a push or pull and cannot describe its effect on motion.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner names movement as the force or says the push remains inside the object after contact ends.
repair_sequence: Name the push or pull during the interaction.
Describe the observed effect without treating force as stored material.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner identifies a fresh push or pull and states its effect on speed, direction or shape.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Assumes rough surfaces always make objects move faster because they provide more grip.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner predicts from the word rough without controlling the object, release or measurement.
repair_sequence: Lock the object and release point.
Run repeated trials on two surfaces.
Use measured distance to make a conclusion limited to the tested setup.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner interprets a new surface comparison from repeated evidence rather than a surface label.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks every metal is attracted to a magnet or that non-metal objects can never contain magnetic material.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner groups by shininess, object name or metal/non-metal appearance instead of test outcome and stated material.
repair_sequence: Test steel, aluminium and copper examples across the same gap.
Group by observed attraction and explain that an object's components matter.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner classifies a new set using test evidence and rejects an all-metals rule.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks magnets attract every object, or that any two magnet ends will attract regardless of pole orientation.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner predicts attraction for non-magnetic material or for matching poles.
repair_sequence: Separate material attraction from magnet-to-magnet interaction.
Reveal and name both facing poles.
Compare N-N, S-S and N-S orientations with towards/away arrows.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner predicts and explains attraction or repulsion for a new labelled orientation.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a quiet virtual lab, muted surfaces, no impact sounds, no sudden magnet snaps and one active test at a time.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace rolling, attracting and repelling animation with numbered still frames, distance values and towards-or-away labels.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Announce setup, changed variable, observation and conclusion in order; replay pole names and table values on request.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use short test cards, icons for change/keep same/measure, glossary popovers and data tables alongside every visual result.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Use a fixed PREDICT-TEST-RECORD-EXPLAIN routine and lock controls unrelated to the current comparison.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Remove timers, allow repeated trials, preserve results and provide side-by-side static comparisons before explanation.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat predictions as testable ideas, reward updating a claim after evidence and keep correct records during repair.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach push, pull, touch, gap, surface, attract, repel, pole and material with gestures, objects and sentence frames.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires accurate surface comparison, magnetic-material classification and pole prediction across at least two formats. Secure requires controlled-variable reasoning, rejection of an all-metals rule and evidence-based attraction or repulsion explanations on two retained sessions.
evidence.parent_summary: Can compare pushes, pulls and surfaces, test which materials a magnet attracts and predict whether labelled magnet poles attract or repel.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Stores prediction, test setup, changed and controlled variables, observation and conclusion separately so scientific reasoning is visible even when reading or motor support is used.
next_actions: Route surface guesses to repeated controlled distance tests.
Route all-metals claims to steel-aluminium-copper evidence comparison.
Extend secure pole reasoning to hidden-pole inference from observed attraction or repulsion.
Keep any real magnet work adult supervised and use age-appropriate magnets too large to swallow.

qa.notes: Quality-hardened Year 3 forces-and-magnets pack with the same four curated variants and 226 deterministic pilot candidates. IDs, answers, blueprint allocation, observations, fair-test tables, pole physics and science scope remain unchanged. Every variant now has evidence-specific correct feedback, a misconception repair route and contact/surface/material/pole model or fair-test support. Explicit touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC/point/adult-recorded routes remove mandatory fine dragging, handwriting and speech. Static sensory-safe alternatives and pressure-free retry remain available. Narration stays selectively absent; any future narration must use produced, human-reviewed ElevenLabs assets and browser TTS is prohibited. Curriculum, teacher, accessibility and safeguarding checks remain required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
